ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for coconut-filled bun; mapped to the broader 'bread product' class as the closest defensible ancestor. Exact term for coconut bun is absent from FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for coconut-filled sweet bun. A000E (Bread and similar products) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for coconut bun as a specific food concept; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard coconut bun weighs approximately 80 g, providing around 308 kcal, and is typically consumed as a single-serve breakfast item or snack.

nutrient_source.title: Coconut Bun — Health Promotion Board Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Coconut Bun (F05020032). All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are taken verbatim from the HPB record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from coconut filling composition using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for desiccated coconut (NDB 12114) and enriched bread dough, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore coconut bun in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 71 is estimated from the closest analogous food: white bread / enriched soft bread roll (GI ~70–73, mean 71) as listed in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The coconut filling adds sugar and fat which may modestly lower the effective GI, but the predominant refined wheat flour matrix drives the high GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (71 × 33.05) / 100 ≈ 23.5, rounded to 23.6 for an 80 g serving (41.31 g carb/100 g × 0.80 = 33.05 g carb per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (18 g/100 g) and moderate sodium (234 mg/100 g) with high saturated fat
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit coconut bun consumption to occasional small portions, as the high sugar load (18 g/100 g) promotes insulin-driven sodium retention and the saturated fat content (estimated 12.5 g/100 g) contributes to endothelial dysfunction; if consumed, pair with unsweetened beverages and avoid other high-sodium foods in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar (18 g/100 g), high GI (~71), and high glycemic load (~23.6 per 80 g serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Coconut bun is unsuitable for regular consumption by individuals with type 2 diabetes due to its high sugar content (18 g/100 g exceeding the 15 g/100 g threshold), high GI of approximately 71, and a glycemic load of ~23.6 per standard serving; if consumed occasionally, limit to half a bun and pair with a protein or high-fibre food to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (estimated 12.5 g/100 g from coconut filling) and possible trace trans fat from margarine
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of coconut bun, as the estimated saturated fat content of approximately 12.5 g/100 g substantially exceeds the AHA recommended daily limit, and the likely presence of trace trans fat from margarine further raises LDL cholesterol and cardiovascular risk; occasional consumption of a half-portion is the maximum advisable intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat from coconut (estimated 12.5 g/100 g) and high sugar (18 g/100 g) promoting systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid coconut bun as the high saturated fat from coconut filling (estimated 12.5 g/100 g, exceeding the 5 g/100 g RA threshold) and high sugar content (18 g/100 g) both promote pro-inflammatory mediators; trace trans fat from margarine further exacerbates joint inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Coconut bun (椰蓉面包, Roti Kelapa) is a popular soft sweet bread roll widely sold in Singapore bakeries, Chinese bakeries across Southeast Asia, and Hong Kong-style cake shops. The bun consists of a lightly enriched yeasted wheat dough — made with flour, sugar, butter or margarine, eggs, milk, and yeast — baked until golden and filled or topped with a sweet desiccated coconut mixture bound with sugar and butter. The texture is pillowy and slightly chewy on the outside with a moist, fragrant, and slightly fibrous coconut filling inside. The flavour profile is mildly sweet with a pronounced toasted coconut aroma. Per 100 g, the bun provides 385 kcal, 7.2 g protein, 21.2 g fat (estimated 12.5 g saturated from coconut and butter), 41.3 g carbohydrate, 18 g sugar, 4 g dietary fibre, and 234 mg sodium. The high saturated fat from coconut filling, high sugar content, and high glycemic index (~71) make this food a high-concern item for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. It is best consumed as an occasional treat rather than a daily staple. Culturally, coconut bun is a common breakfast and snack item in Singapore, Malaysia, and Hong Kong, often purchased from traditional Chinese bakeries or pasar malam stalls. It is vegetarian and typically halal-certified in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a coconut bun?
Is coconut bun high in sugar?
Can I eat coconut bun if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 with an exact record match (F05020032). Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for desiccated coconut and enriched bread dough, which is an approved secondary source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using white bread / soft enriched roll as the closest match. Overall confidence is rated high given the primary HPB source match and transparent estimation for derived fields.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (12.5 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for desiccated coconut (NDB 12114) and enriched bread dough, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item. This is an approximation and may vary by recipe. 2. Trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) is a trace estimate based on the likely use of margarine in commercial coconut bun production; this value is not confirmed by HPB and should be verified against product ingredient labels. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient, and moisture data for this specific coconut bun preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 4. GI of 71 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using soft white bread roll as the closest approved-source equivalent; no direct GI measurement for Singapore coconut bun was found. 5. The dietary tag 'plant_based' from the input record is not carried forward as the bun contains eggs and dairy (milk, butter), making it lacto-ovo vegetarian but not plant-based or vegan. 6. Halal status is assumed based on typical Singapore bakery certification; consumers should verify with the specific vendor.
